content.text: Writing tests before writing the code they verify is the defining inversion of test-driven development. Rather than building a feature and then confirming it works, the developer first describes the desired behaviour in executable form — a test that currently fails — and then writes only enough production code to make that test pass. The workflow repeats in short cycles until the full feature is implemented.

In the context of agentic AI systems, the technique adapts in a specific way. An agent's observable behaviour is not just its final text output — it is a series of decisions: which tool to call, what to pass as arguments, whether to retry after an error, when to stop looping and return a result. These decision points are precisely what TDD is designed to pin down. Each test documents one decision. The LLM that drives the agent is replaced by a mock that returns a controlled response, making the test deterministic even though the real model is probabilistic.

The resulting test suite is an executable specification of the agent. When someone reads the test file, they immediately understand every behavioural contract the agent is expected to satisfy. When the suite stays green, that contract is being honoured. The tests give the development team the confidence to refactor internals, upgrade dependencies, or swap prompt templates without silently breaking the agent's logic.

Agentic systems are harder to reason about than conventional software. An agent may loop across multiple LLM calls, branch based on which tool the model chooses to invoke, and maintain a growing conversation history. The number of possible execution paths grows quickly, and a change to any part of the stack — a prompt edit, a new tool, a different model — can silently alter behaviour in a path that is never manually exercised.

Without a test suite, the team's only safeguard is running the agent against real inputs and reading its outputs. That process is slow, expensive (each run consumes API tokens), non-deterministic (the model may take different branches on successive runs with the same input), and unscalable — manual verification cannot keep pace with feature development. Bugs that would take two minutes to find in a test suite survive until they reach production.

TDD prevents this by locking in each behavioural decision as it is implemented. The failing test is written first, which means the developer must think through the expected agent behaviour before touching production code. That discipline surfaces design problems early: if the expected behaviour is hard to describe in a test, the agent's interface is probably too complicated.
Every test is a precise, machine-verifiable statement of what the agent must do. Reading the test file tells a new developer exactly how the agent is supposed to behave in each scenario.
Once a behaviour is captured in a test, any future change that breaks it surfaces immediately. Without TDD, regressions are discovered manually, often much later and at greater cost.

Writing the test first forces the developer to define the agent's public interface before implementation. Interfaces that are hard to test are usually badly designed. TDD surfaces these problems when they are cheapest to fix.
An agent with a comprehensive test suite can be refactored aggressively. The developer changes internals, runs the suite, and gets immediate feedback. Without tests, refactoring is guesswork.

When a test fails, the failing test pinpoints which specific behaviour broke. Debugging without tests requires manually reconstructing the scenario — a process that can take hours for a complex agent.
TDD makes it impossible to avoid properly injecting the LLM client and tool callables, because untestable code cannot satisfy the failing test. The test-first discipline enforces good architecture.
Unlike a README or design doc, tests fail when they are out of date. The test suite is the only documentation guaranteed to reflect the current behaviour of the agent.

The primary cost of TDD is the upfront investment. Writing a test before the implementation takes more discipline and more time in the short term. Developers unfamiliar with the technique find the first few cycles uncomfortable, because the instinct is to code first and verify second. The productivity benefit — faster debugging, cheaper refactoring, fewer regressions — accrues over time rather than immediately.
Each feature requires authoring a test before writing any production code. In early sprints, velocity appears lower. The payback comes when the suite prevents regressions in later sprints.

Agentic TDD relies on mocking the LLM client and tool callables. Those mocks must match the real interfaces. When the Anthropic SDK ships a new response shape, mocks need updating. This work must be tracked and scheduled.

A test that asserts every argument in every API call becomes brittle. Changing a non-behavioural detail — the model name, the max_tokens value — breaks the test even though the logic is correct. Tests should assert outcomes, not implementation details.
TDD tests agent logic with mocked LLM responses. They do not test whether the real model produces the right answers. Evaluation harnesses are needed for that orthogonal concern.

Agents that instantiate their own clients internally cannot easily be tested. Retrofitting TDD onto an existing codebase that was not designed for it requires refactoring before the tests can be written.

The TDD cycle has three named phases: Red, Green, and Refactor. The names come from the test-runner status indicator that most tools display during a suite run. Understanding each phase precisely avoids the most common misapplication of the technique — writing code first and retroactively writing tests to match.

Before writing any production code, the developer writes a test that asserts the new behaviour. For an agentic system, this test configures a mock LLM client to return a specific response and then asserts that the agent calls the right tool, produces the right output, or handles an error correctly. The test is run immediately — and must fail. Seeing the failure confirms two things: the test is actually testing something (a test that passes without any implementation is a false positive), and the failure message makes sense as a description of what is missing.
The developer writes the smallest amount of production code required to make the failing test pass. The emphasis on

is deliberate. Adding more than the test demands means writing code without test coverage, which reintroduces the regression risk that TDD is designed to eliminate. If the agent needs two behaviours, two separate TDD cycles are run. After each implementation, the full suite is run — not just the new test — to confirm that no existing test was broken.

Once all tests are green, the developer can safely clean up the code: extract helper functions, rename variables, remove duplication, improve the prompt template structure. The tests remain green throughout. Because the refactor step happens only after the tests are green, it is safe to make structural changes with confidence. If the refactor accidentally breaks behaviour, the suite immediately goes red. This is the safety net that enables aggressive improvement of internal code quality.

The cycle repeats for every new behaviour. In practice, each cycle takes a few minutes for a simple agentic behaviour — writing the test, running it to see red, adding the routing logic or error handler, and running to see green. Complex multi-turn agent behaviours may involve several interlocking cycles before the feature is complete.

attrs.alt: TDD cycle for agentic AI: Red (Write Test) leads to Run FAIL, then Implement, then Run GREEN, then Refactor, and back to Write Test

content.text: The five stages form a closed loop. Stage 1 (Write Test, red border) captures a new behavioural requirement before any production code exists. Stage 2 confirms that the suite is actually red — a test that passes without implementation is vacuous. Stage 3 adds the minimum implementation. Stage 4 confirms the suite turns green with no existing test broken. Stage 5 (Refactor, amber border) improves internal code quality while the suite enforces that behaviour is unchanged. The dashed arrow from Refactor back to Write Test marks the start of the next feature cycle.

TDD is most valuable when the agent's logic — not the model's intelligence — is what needs to be built correctly. Any behaviour that can be specified as an input-output contract is a good TDD candidate: call tool X when the LLM produces response Y; retry when a rate-limit error occurs; return an error when the tool call result is empty; stop looping after N iterations.

implementing a new agent feature that has a clear behavioural specification, such as a routing rule (if the model requests web_search, call the search tool and append the results) or an error handler (if the API returns a 429, wait and retry up to three times).

refactoring an existing agent. Writing tests that cover the current behaviour before starting the refactor ensures the refactor does not change that behaviour. This use of TDD after the fact is called characterisation testing.

a production bug is reported. Write a test that reproduces the bug before fixing it. The test turns red (confirming the bug is reproduced), then the fix turns it green. The bug can never silently return.
the goal is to evaluate whether the model produces good answers. Prompt quality is measured by evaluation harnesses that run against the real LLM, not by mock-based unit tests.

rapidly prototyping an agent to explore whether a given approach is feasible. During the exploratory phase, the requirements change too quickly for a test suite to track. Switch to TDD once the design stabilises.
The TDD suite tests logic with mocks. A small number of integration tests that make real API calls should be run on a schedule to confirm that the mocks accurately reflect the live API.
Applying TDD to an agentic AI project requires a specific sequence of setup steps before the first cycle can run. The setup is a one-time investment that pays back over the lifetime of the project.
The agent must accept its LLM client and tool callables as constructor arguments, not create them internally. This is the prerequisite for all TDD in agentic AI. An agent that calls
inside its own body cannot be tested without patching module internals.
for canned LLM response JSON files. Install
with shared fixtures: a mock Anthropic client, helper functions to build fake LLM response objects, and stubs for every external tool the agent calls.

Identify one specific behaviour the agent must have. Write a test function that instantiates the agent with the mock client, configures the mock to return a specific response, calls the agent, and asserts on the output or the mock call log.
Run the test and confirm red.
and confirm that the new test fails. If it passes without implementation, the test is not testing anything useful.
Add only the production code required to satisfy the failing test. Resist the urge to add extra logic for future tests.
Run the suite and confirm green.
All tests, not just the new one, must pass. A newly passing test that breaks an existing one indicates an unanticipated interaction in the implementation.
If the implementation introduced duplication or poor naming, clean it up now while all tests are green. Re-run the suite after each refactoring step.
Write the next failing test and run a new cycle. A mature agentic agent is built from many small cycles, each adding one testable behaviour.
Cycle 1 — Test-first tool-routing decision (Red and Green phases)
The first cycle specifies that the agent must call the

tool when the mock LLM returns a tool-use block requesting it. The test is written before any agent class exists — running it immediately will fail with an import error, which is the correct Red state.

# tests/test_agent_routing.py
# RED PHASE — written before ResearchAgent exists.
# pip install pytest anthropic

from unittest.mock import MagicMock

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Helper builders (normally live in tests/conftest.py)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

def make_tool_use_response(tool_name: str, tool_id: str, tool_input: dict) -> MagicMock:
    """Build a fake messages.create() response that requests a tool call."""
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "tool_use"
    block.name = tool_name
    block.id = tool_id
    block.input = tool_input

    response = MagicMock()
    response.stop_reason = "tool_use"
    response.content = [block]
    return response


def make_text_response(text: str) -> MagicMock:
    """Build a fake messages.create() response with a final text answer."""
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "text"
    block.text = text

    response = MagicMock()
    response.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    response.content = [block]
    return response


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Tests — all RED until ResearchAgent is implemented
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

class TestAgentRouting:
    def test_agent_calls_search_tool_when_llm_requests_it(self):
        from agent.research_agent import ResearchAgent  # ImportError until Green

        mock_client = MagicMock()
        mock_search = MagicMock(return_value=[{"title": "Result", "snippet": "A"}])

        # LLM: first call returns tool-use, second returns final answer
        mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = [
            make_tool_use_response("web_search", "tu_001", {"query": "Python 3.13"}),
            make_text_response("Python 3.13 was released in October 2024."),
        ]

        agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_client, tools={"web_search": mock_search})
        result = agent.run("What is the latest Python release?")

        mock_search.assert_called_once_with(query="Python 3.13")
        assert "3.13" in result

    def test_agent_returns_text_without_tool_call(self):
        from agent.research_agent import ResearchAgent

        mock_client = MagicMock()
        mock_search = MagicMock()
        mock_client.messages.create.return_value = make_text_response("42 is the answer.")

        agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_client, tools={"web_search": mock_search})
        result = agent.run("What is the answer to life?")

        assert result == "42 is the answer."
        mock_search.assert_not_called()
. That is the Red state. The Green phase writes the minimal agent class:
Cycle 2 — Error handling and retry logic

# tests/test_agent_retry.py
# Second TDD cycle: agent must retry on transient errors.

import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock


def make_text_response(text: str) -> MagicMock:
    block = MagicMock()
    block.type = "text"
    block.text = text
    response = MagicMock()
    response.stop_reason = "end_turn"
    response.content = [block]
    return response


class TestAgentRetry:
    def test_agent_retries_once_on_transient_error(self):
        """Agent with max_retries=1 must succeed on the second attempt."""
        from agent.research_agent import ResearchAgent

        mock_client = MagicMock()
        mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = [
            Exception("transient error"),
            make_text_response("Retry succeeded."),
        ]

        agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_client, tools={}, max_retries=1)
        result = agent.run("Any question")

        assert result == "Retry succeeded."
        assert mock_client.messages.create.call_count == 2

    def test_agent_raises_after_max_retries_exceeded(self):
        """When every attempt fails, the agent must propagate the exception."""
        from agent.research_agent import ResearchAgent

        mock_client = MagicMock()
        mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = Exception("persistent error")

        agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_client, tools={}, max_retries=2)

        with pytest.raises(Exception, match="persistent error"):
            agent.run("Any question")

        assert mock_client.messages.create.call_count == 3  # original + 2 retries

    @pytest.mark.parametrize("max_retries", [0, 1, 3])
    def test_retry_count_matches_configuration(self, max_retries: int):
        """Call count must equal max_retries + 1 regardless of the configured value."""
        from agent.research_agent import ResearchAgent

        mock_client = MagicMock()
        mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = Exception("always fails")

        agent = ResearchAgent(client=mock_client, tools={}, max_retries=max_retries)

        with pytest.raises(Exception):
            agent.run("question")

        assert mock_client.messages.create.call_count == max_retries + 1

    def test_loop_terminates_after_max_tool_turns(self):
        """
        An agent that receives tool-use on every call must stop after a
        configurable maximum number of turns rather than loop forever.
        """
        from agent.research_agent import ResearchAgent

        mock_client = MagicMock()

        # Always return a tool-use block — never end_turn
        def always_tool_use():
            block = MagicMock()
            block.type = "tool_use"
            block.name = "web_search"
            block.id = "tu_x"
            block.input = {"query": "test"}
            r = MagicMock()
            r.stop_reason = "tool_use"
            r.content = [block]
            return r

        mock_client.messages.create.side_effect = always_tool_use
        mock_search = MagicMock(return_value=[{"title": "x", "snippet": "y"}])

        agent = ResearchAgent(
            client=mock_client,
            tools={"web_search": mock_search},
            max_tool_turns=3,
        )

        # The agent must return (or raise) rather than loop indefinitely.
        # ResearchAgent.__init__ will need max_tool_turns — this test drives
        # that parameter into existence on the next Green phase.
        result = agent.run("Infinite question")
        assert mock_client.messages.create.call_count <= 3

Consider a production customer-support agent that handles two intent types: order status lookups (which require a database tool call) and general questions (which the model answers directly from its knowledge). The agent is built entirely test-first over three sprints. After twelve TDD cycles, the test suite contains 24 tests covering happy paths, error paths, and edge cases.
During sprint four, the team discovers that the model occasionally requests a tool not in the registry. Without a test for that case, the agent throws an unhandled
in production. The fix is a TDD cycle: write a test that configures the mock to return a tool-use block with an unregistered tool name, confirm the test is red, add a

handler that returns an error message to the model, confirm the test turns green. The bug is fixed with a reproducible test that guarantees it cannot regress. The suite now has 25 tests and the fix is deployed the same afternoon.

Six months later, the team upgrades models. Three tests fail because the new model sometimes returns two tool-use blocks in a single response, while the old implementation only processed the first one. The failing tests identify the exact behaviour that needs updating. The team adjusts the loop, runs the suite, and all 25 tests pass. The upgrade ships in one day instead of three days of manual verification.

The most common misapplication is writing the implementation first and adding tests afterwards to achieve coverage. Tests written after the fact tend to mirror the implementation rather than specify behaviour — they assert what the code does, not what it should do. A refactoring that changes internal structure without changing external behaviour breaks these tests even though nothing went wrong.

Failing to run the suite after writing the test but before writing the implementation means never confirming the test is valid. A test that passes even without any production code is a false positive — it provides confidence without verification. Always run the suite after writing the test and confirm a new failure appears in the output.

break whenever any argument changes, even when the change is unrelated to the behaviour under test. Assert on the outcome — what the agent returned, which tool callable it invoked — rather than on every parameter passed to the LLM client.

Adding more than one new behaviour per Green phase bypasses the discipline of minimal implementation. Code written beyond what the failing test demands lacks test coverage. The next cycle's test may pass or fail for the wrong reasons, making failures hard to diagnose. Each cycle adds exactly one new behaviour.

A test that inspects the agent's internal message history structure after a tool call is testing an implementation detail. If the agent is refactored to store history differently while producing identical output, the test breaks unnecessarily. Tests should assert on the agent's public interface: return values, tool invocations, and raised exceptions.
One failing test at a time.
Never have more than one red test simultaneously. The discipline of one cycle at a time keeps the development loop tight and makes failures easy to diagnose.
. A descriptive name doubles as documentation and makes failure messages immediately informative.
A test function should be readable in under ten seconds. Setup shared across multiple tests belongs in a
Assert on outcomes, not on internals.

Check what the agent returns and which callables it invoked. Avoid inspecting private attributes, internal message history structure, or the exact sequence of mock calls unless that sequence is itself the specified behaviour.
For every error the agent is expected to handle — rate limits, tool not found, empty model response, malformed JSON — write a dedicated test that injects that error via
and asserts on the correct recovery behaviour.
Run the suite before every commit.
A failing test should never be committed. Set up a pre-commit hook or CI check that blocks merges when the suite is red.
Use parametrize to cover boundary conditions.
lets a single test body cover many input variations. This is especially useful for testing the agent's loop termination: test that it stops after exactly one, two, and N tool turns.
Pair TDD tests with a small integration suite.

TDD tests use mocks and run in milliseconds. A separate, scheduled integration suite makes real API calls to confirm that mock shapes remain accurate. Keep the integration suite small and run it nightly or before release.

A TDD suite for an agentic AI project is fast by construction. Because every test uses mock clients instead of real API calls, there is no network I/O. The entire test suite for a mature agent with 50 behavioural tests typically runs in under five seconds on a laptop. This speed means the suite can be run on every file save during development without interrupting the flow.

As the agent grows, the suite scales linearly: each new behaviour adds roughly one to three tests. A test file with 200 tests is not unusual for a production agent. At that scale, run time remains well under a minute because each test is purely in-memory computation. The following table summarises the scaling characteristics:
Because mocked tests share no real external state, they are safe to run in parallel with
). On a four-core machine, this typically cuts wall-clock time by 60 to 70 percent for large suites.
for read-only, immutable test data loaded from JSON fixture files. Use the default
for mocks that record call state, to prevent one test's call log from leaking into the next.
Cache the virtual environment in CI. Installing
on every push is the dominant time cost when the suite itself runs in five seconds. With a warm dependency cache, the total CI job time is typically under 30 seconds.

instances record every call made on them. A test that drives an agent through many LLM turns accumulates a large call log. Use function-scoped fixtures so each test starts with a fresh mock, releasing the prior test's memory.
The de-facto Python test framework. Provides
(required when the LLM client is used in async code). No installation required.
Parallel test execution across multiple CPU cores.
Measures how much of the production code is exercised by the test suite. Run
. A TDD-driven codebase typically achieves 90 to 100 percent coverage naturally because every line was introduced to make a test pass.

An HTTPX-level mock library that intercepts real HTTP requests from the Anthropic SDK. Useful for testing streaming responses or for projects that prefer transport-level mocking rather than Python-object patching.

Property-based testing that generates thousands of random inputs to find edge cases the developer did not think to test. Complements TDD for functions that parse or transform data, such as the agent's tool-call result formatter.
A framework for defining git hooks. Configure
as a pre-commit hook so the suite runs automatically before every commit, preventing red tests from reaching the repository.
High — every behaviour is covered from the start
Low — tests mirror code, not requirements
Low per feature, higher in bugs over time
Very high — Gherkin syntax plus step definitions
Low — does not drive design decisions
None — tests model quality, not code logic
High — requires real LLM API calls every run

TDD is the strongest choice for building agent logic correctly and maintaining it safely over time. BDD adds a natural-language layer valuable when non-technical stakeholders read the specifications. Property-based testing complements TDD for functions that transform structured data. Evaluation harnesses serve a different purpose — measuring model quality — and do not substitute for TDD.

A test suite built with TDD is itself a reliability asset. Every critical agent behaviour has a corresponding test that runs in CI. A deployment that would break a critical behaviour is caught before it reaches production. This structural protection is especially valuable in agentic AI systems, where a broken routing decision or missing error handler can result in the agent silently doing nothing, calling the wrong tool, or entering an infinite loop.

An agent that loops on LLM calls must have a maximum iteration count. Write a TDD test that configures the mock to always return tool-use responses and asserts that the agent raises an exception or returns after the configured maximum number of turns. This test prevents runaway loops in production.
Write a test that injects a tool-use block requesting a tool not in the registry and asserts that the agent handles it gracefully. An unhandled
in production causes a hard crash that the user sees as a timeout.
TDD tests require no API keys because they mock all API calls. Ensure that test files contain no real API keys, real user data, or real tool outputs that might contain personal information.
everywhere. If a new SDK version removes an attribute that the agent accesses, a spec-constrained mock will raise
silently accepts any attribute access, allowing the test to pass while the real code is broken.
Configure the CI pipeline to block pull-request merges when the TDD suite fails. A green suite in CI is the deployment gatekeeper. If a merge can bypass the suite, the protection is lost.

Think of TDD as building a bridge with a load-test rig bolted to every span. Before pouring any concrete (writing production code), an engineer specifies the load requirements for the next span in writing (the test). The span is then poured to meet exactly that specification. Immediately after the concrete sets, the load rig runs. If the span holds, the rig stays in place permanently and every future pour must not crack it. If the span fails, the pour is redesigned and tried again.

The Refactor phase is equivalent to resurfacing the road after all spans are poured — cleaning and smoothing without adding new spans or changing the load-bearing structure. The load rigs do not care about the surface texture; they care only about load capacity. As long as all rigs pass, the resurfacing is safe.

For agentic AI, the concrete is the agent's routing logic, error handling, and loop control. The load rig is the mock-based test. The LLM is the unpredictable weather — not the subject of the test, but isolated from it so it cannot cause a false reading.
TDD inverts the usual order: write a failing test first, then write the minimum code to pass it, then refactor — always in that sequence and never out of order.
The Red step is not ceremonial: running the suite before writing implementation confirms the test is real and catches tests that would pass vacuously without any code.
Agentic AI TDD mocks the LLM client and tool callables, making tests deterministic and free of API cost regardless of how non-deterministic the real model is.

TDD forces dependency injection: an agent must accept its client and tools as constructor arguments before a test can instantiate it with mocks — a constraint that improves architecture across the board.

Assert on outcomes — return values and tool invocations — rather than on mock call arguments or internal state; over-specified assertions become a maintenance burden that makes the suite fragile to innocent changes.
A TDD-driven agent suite runs in seconds, requires no API keys, and scales linearly as the agent grows; run it on every save during development and block merges on failure in CI.
TDD tests agent logic; evaluation harnesses test model quality — both are necessary and neither substitutes for the other.
